text: The U.S. Treasury 10-year par yield was 4.47% on June 15, 2026, down 1 basis point from 4.48% on June 12, 2026, per the official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve. Despite the broad equity rally, the belly-to-long end of the curve barely moved as falling oil eased the forward inflation impulse ahead of the June 16-17 FOMC.
The U.S. Treasury 2-year par yield eased to 4.07% on June 15, 2026 from 4.09% on June 12, 2026, a 2 basis point decline that left the policy-sensitive front end modestly lower into the FOMC decision.
The U.S. Treasury 30-year par yield was unchanged at 4.97% on June 15, 2026 versus June 12, 2026, anchoring the long end even as risk appetite surged.

The 2s10s Treasury par-yield spread was +40 basis points on June 15, 2026 (10Y 4.47% minus 2Y 4.07%), a 1 basis point steepening from +39 basis points on June 12, as the 2-year fell slightly more than the 10-year in a mild bull steepening.

In secondary-market trading the 10-year Treasury note yield slid to roughly 4.43% on June 15, 2026, its lowest in about a month, as the U.S.-Iran peace deal and the resulting drop in oil prices drove a rethink of near-term Fed rate-hike risk, per CNBC. The secondary-market yield sits below the 4.47% official par yield because the two series are constructed differently.

WTI crude oil fell to about $80.23 per barrel on June 15, 2026, down 5.48% on the day, per Trading Economics, after the U.S. and Iran reached a peace agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and restore Persian Gulf oil flows.

Brent crude fell to about $83.01 per barrel on June 15, 2026, down 4.95% on the day and near a two-month low, per Trading Economics, as the Middle East risk premium drained out of the oil market following the peace agreement.

Gold rose to about $4,338.90 per troy ounce on June 15, 2026, up 2.77% on the day, per Trading Economics, as lower Treasury yields and a softer dollar supported the metal ahead of a week of major central-bank decisions, even with equities rallying.

The U.S. Dollar Index slipped to about 99.56 on June 15, 2026, down 0.19% and its lowest in more than a week, per Trading Economics, as the easing of Middle East tensions curbed safe-haven demand for the dollar.
Heading into June 15, 2026 the federal funds target range stood at 3.50%-3.75%, with the next FOMC decision due Wednesday, June 17, 2026 at the close of the two-day June 16-17 meeting.

Market pricing put roughly an 89% probability on the Fed holding rates at the June 17, 2026 decision, versus about 9% for a 25 basis point cut, per Polymarket, framing the meeting as a likely hold with attention on any shift in policy bias.

As the macro risk barometer, the S&P 500 closed at 7,554.29 on June 15, 2026, up about 1.65%, with the global risk-on move led by a 3.1% jump in the Nasdaq Composite and a near-5% surge in Japan's Nikkei 225, all driven by the U.S.-Iran peace deal and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.
